coredata.dc:title: Formation of N, S-codoped fluorescent carbon dots from biomass and their application for the selective detection of mercury and iron ion
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Ye, Qianghua
Yan, Fanyong
Luo, Yunmei
Wang, Yinyin
Zhou, Xuguang
Chen, Li
coredata.dc:description: Biomass is regarded as an excellent candidate for the preparation of heteroatom-doped carbon nanomaterials. We have developed a simple and facile one-pot synthesis of nitrogen and sulfur codoped fluorescent carbon dots from pigeon feathers, egg and manure via the pyrolysis carbonization method. The as-prepared four PCDs have high fluorescence quantum yield about 24.87% (PCDs-f), 17.48% (PCDs-w), 16.34% (PCDs-y), 33.50% (PCDs-m), respectively, which is higher than the other carbon dots preparing from biomass. We found that the preparation of PCDs-m with pigeon manure has no favourable selectively with heavy metal ions. However, other PCDs exhibit highly sensitive and selective detection behavior of Hg2+/Fe3+ ions with a low detection limit of 10.3 and 60.9nM. They were applied to imaging of human umbilical vein endothelial cells, showing low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Biomass
Carbon dots
Ion detection
Fluorescent
Bioimaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence Detection for Hg2+ and Fe3+
The Mechanism of the Hg2+ and Fe3+ Detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The TEM images of (a) PCDs-f, (b) PCDs-w, (c) PCDs-y, and (d) PCDs-m.
FTIR spectrum of PCDs: (a) PCDs-f, (b) PCDs-w, (c) PCDs-y, and (d) PCDs-m.
(a) XPS survey spectrum, and (b) C 1s, (c) N 1s, and (d) S 2p high-resolution XPS spectra of the PCDs-m.
PCDs solution under white (left) and 365nm UV (right) light illumination: (a) PCDs-f, (b) PCDs-w, (c) PCDs-y, (d) PCDs-m and (e) UV–Vis absorption spectra of the PCDs.
Fluorescence emission spectra of the PCDs obtained at different excitation wavelengths with a 10nm increment from 300 to 400nm, (a) PCDs-f, (b) PCDs-w, (c) PCDs-y, (d) PCDs-m.
The fluorescence intensities of PCDs at 400nm with different pH values (a) and time (b).
Effect of metal ions on the fluorescence of PCDs: (a) PCDs-f (Ex=330nm), (b) PCDs-w (Ex=340nm), (c) PCDs-y (Ex=340nm), (d) PCDs-m (Ex=350nm).

The fluorescence spectra of PCDs-f with different concentrations of Hg2+ (a) and Fe3+ (c) from 0 to 1.2μM, respectively. The relationship between the F/F0 and Hg2+/Fe3+ concentrations (b: 0–0.12μM; d: 0–1.6μM) by fitting to the Stern–Volmer equation.
The fluorescence intensities of PCDs-f in Tris-HCl buffer and HEPES buffer in water containing 0.5μM Hg2+ and Fe3+, respectively.

Bright-field transmission image (A) and fluorescence image (B) of HUVEC cells incubated with 2mgmL−1 PCDs-f for 1h at 37°C. Bright-field transmission image (C) and fluorescence image (D) of HUVEC cells which were first incubated with 2mgmL−1 PCDs-f for 1h and then incubated with 2μM Hg2+ for about ∼30min at 37°C.
Schematic illustration of the preparation and application of fluorescent PCDs from pigeon biomass.
The QY of CDs using biomass as precursor.
Comparison of different fluorescent PCDs for Hg2+ and Fe3+ detection.

head.ce:title: Formation of N, S-codoped fluorescent carbon dots from biomass and their application for the selective detection of mercury and iron ion

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Separation Membranes and Membrane Processes, School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering, Tianjin Polytechnic University, Tianjin 300387, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Separation Membranes and Membrane Processes
School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmacology, Key Laboratory for Basic Pharmacology of Ministry of Education, Zunyi Medical College, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for Basic Pharmacology of Ministry of Education

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering, Tianjin Polytechnic University, Tianjin 300387, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These two authors contributed equally to this paper, and should share first authorship.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Biomass is regarded as an excellent candidate for the preparation of heteroatom-doped carbon nanomaterials. We have developed a simple and facile one-pot synthesis of nitrogen and sulfur codoped fluorescent carbon dots from pigeon feathers, egg and manure via the pyrolysis carbonization method. The as-prepared four PCDs have high fluorescence quantum yield about 24.87% (PCDs-f), 17.48% (PCDs-w), 16.34% (PCDs-y), 33.50% (PCDs-m), respectively, which is higher than the other carbon dots preparing from biomass. We found that the preparation of PCDs-m with pigeon manure has no favourable selectively with heavy metal ions. However, other PCDs exhibit highly sensitive and selective detection behavior of Hg2+/Fe3+ ions with a low detection limit of 10.3 and 60.9nM. They were applied to imaging of human umbilical vein endothelial cells, showing low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility.

ce:section.ce:para: Mercury ions, especially water-soluble Hg2+, can easily pass through skin, respiratory and gastrointestinal tissues, leading to DNA damages, mitosis impairment and permanent damages to the central nervous system [1–3]. Iron ion is the most abundant transition metal ions in the human body and the overdose of iron is known to cause serious problems, such as liver damage, kidney failure or even death [4,5]. As a result, there has been ongoing efforts to develop better sensors for the detection of mercury and iron, due to their significance in the environment and in biological systems.

In the past decade, carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as a new class of extraordinarily bright fluorescent probes. With its outstanding properties such as high aqueous solubility, good biocompatibility, low toxicity and environmental-friendly, good sensitivity and selectivity, high reliability compared with organic dyes and conventional semiconductor quantum dots [6–10]. CDs have emerged into a rising star applied in wide range fields including bioimaging [11–13], catalysis [14,15] and sensor [8,16]. For ion sensors, especially mercury ion and iron sensor, several previous reports have tried to apply CDs from biomass for fluorescence detection of Hg2+ and Fe3+. Liu et al. [17] reported nitrogen and sulfur doped CDs from goose feathers with fluorescence quantum yield up to 17.1% and exhibited highly sensitive and selective detection behavior of Fe3+ ions with a low detection limit. Yuan et al. [18] prepared nitrogen-doped CDs from plant cytoplasm detecting P-nitroaniline in both aqueous and soils owing to a proposed hydrogen-bonding effect. Yu et al. [19] prepared green, low-cost, and water-soluble fluorescent CDs to sensitive and selective fluorescent detection of Hg2+ and Fe3+ by Jinhua bergamot. Moreover, good and promising application performance of CDs always involves successive functionalization, surface passivation and heteroatom doping after/in synthesis procedure, which improve performance.

Renewable resources as precursor have been developed to prepare CDs such as hair [20], banana juice [21], bee pollens [22], potato [23] and winter melon [24]. Therefore, the exploration of new carbon source for simple, economical and green synthesis of fluorescent CDs is highly desirable. Nowadays, using biomass to prepare carbon nanomaterials has been a trend to synthesize heteroatoms doped material because biomass contains abundant heteroatoms.

Feather is rich in carbon, nitrogen, sulfur and oxygen elements because it is composed of the β-keratin. Eggs are a usual aliment in our daily life with plentiful proteins, carbohydrate, and fat that contain a lot of heteroatoms. We think that the manufacturing of heteroatom-doped carbon nanomaterials with feather and egg as the precursors will be experimentally feasible, which can't be used organic chemicals in a single step and it conforms to the concept of green chemistry. In this work, we demonstrate the first use of low cost wastes of pigeon feathers, egg and manure, as a carbon source for green preparation of water soluble fluorescent PCDs by pyrolysis. We observed that photoluminescent (PL) emission band of the as-prepared PCDs shift with the change of excitation wavelength and exhibit high fluorescence quantum yield, compared with other reported biomass CDs. Besides, we further demonstrate that such PCDs can serve as a very effective fluorescent probe for label-free, sensitive and selective detection of Hg2+ and Fe3+. The preparation and application procedure were illustrated in Scheme. 1 .

Pigeon feathers, pigeon egg, pigeon manure, goose egg and ethanol purchased from the local company. Quinine sulfate (99%, suitable for fluorescence) was supplied by Aladdin. All other reagents were of analytical grade and used as received. The solutions of metal ions were prepared from NaNO3, KNO3, AgNO3, MgSO4, CaCl2, Mn(NO3)2·4H2O, Co(NO3)2·6H2O, Ni(NO3)2·6H2O, CuCl2·2H2O, Zn(NO3)2·6H2O, Cd(NO3)2·2H2O, Ba(NO3)2, PdCl2, Hg(NO3)2·0.5H2O, Pb(NO3)2, Fe(NO3)3·9H2O, Ce(NO3)3·6H2O and Al(NO3)3·9H2O.

The FTIR spectra were recorded on a fourier-transform infrared spectrometer (TENSOR37). The XPS spectra measurements were performed on an X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (EDAX, GENESIS 60S). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) measurements were performed on an electronic microscope (HITACHI, H-7650). The absorption spectra of the samples were measured on a UV–Vis spectrometer (Purkinje, General TU-1901). Fluorescence spectra were obtained by an F-380 fluorescence spectrophotometer. The fluorescence imaging was observed under an OlympusIX71 inverted fluorescence microscope with a 20× objective lens.

The PCDs were synthesized by modifying the previously reported pyrolysis method. In the typical experiment, pigeon feathers (PCDs-f), pigeon egg (pigeon egg white as PCDs-w and pigeon egg yolk as PCDs-y), pigeon manure (PCDs-m), goose egg (goose egg white as GCDs-w and goose egg yolk as GCDs-w) are significant precursors. The CDs was obtained by directly heating egg white, egg yolk, pigeon feathers or pigeon manure (5g precursor in a 30mL crucible) in a muffle furnace in air under certain calcination parameter settings as follows: from room temperature to 300°C and it was finally heated at 300°C for 3h, respectively. After the reaction completed, the crucible was cooled down to room temperature, the resulting black powder was dissolved in ultra-pure water, the solution was condensed by rotary evaporation. After removing the insoluble precipitate by centrifugation and the supernatant liquid was then loaded into dialysis using a semipermeable membrane (MWCO 1000) for dialysis against distilled water for 4days. The final product was obtained by rotary evaporation and the obtained product was PCDs.

Based on the mechanism of fluorescence destroy, we used the as-prepared PCDs and GCDs for metal ions detection. Firstly we connected various metal ions solution such as AgNO3, Ni(NO3)2, Pb(NO3)2, Zn(NO3)2, Cd(NO3)2 and so on. In a typical detection experiment, 50μL PCDs and GCDs solution were added into a certain amount of metal ions solution (0.002molL−1) to form a 10mL clear solution. After mixing evenly, fluorescence measurements were carried out with excitation and mission slit width of 5 and 5nm and excitation wavelength was 340/350nm.

The quenching of fluorescence by Hg2+ or Fe3+ was described using the Stern-Volmer equation:
(3)
F 0
/ F = 1 +
K
sv
C
where KSV is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant, C the concentration of Hg2+ or Fe3+, F0 and F the PL intensity of PCDs without Hg2+/Fe3+ and with different concentration of Hg2+/Fe3+, respectively. The detection limit used the equation 3σ/m, where σ is the relative standard deviation and m the slope of calibration curve as described in detail [25].

Human umbilical vein endothelial cells (HUVEC) were cultured in (Invitrogen) supplemented with 10% Fetal Bovine Serum (FBS, Invitrogen) in an atmosphere of 5% CO2 at 37°C. The cells were then incubated with the 2mgmL−1 PCDs-f in the culture medium for 1h, and the medium was removed and the cells washed with PBS 3 times. Finally, 1mL of PBS was added to the plate and the cells were observed under inverted fluorescence microscopy.

To evaluate cytotoxicity of PCDs was taken as an example to perform a MTT assay on HUVEC cells with PCDs-f concentrations from 0 to 200μgmL−1. A cytotoxicity screen was conducted for four PCDs-f concentrations: 0, 50, 100, 200μgmL−1. The cellular viability estimated was ca. 96% in 24h after treatment with 200μgmL−1 of PCDs-f, exhibiting low toxicity to HUVEC cells.

Fig. 1 shows the TEM image of the obtained PCDs, all the samples have a typical spherical shape with narrow size distribution. Based on the statistical analysis of particles, the average diameter of PCDs-f, PCDs-w, PCDs-y and PCDs-m were 3.8±0.5, 3.3±0.5, 3.2±0.5 and 4.2±0.5nm, respectively (Fig. S1).

Chemical and structural information of the PCDs were further obtained from the FTIR spectrum. As illustrated in Fig. 2 , the obtained PCDs exhibit characteristic absorption bands of OH and NH stretching vibrations at 3450cm−1, the absorption band at about 3150cm−1 is assigned to the unsaturated CH stretching and the peak near 1570cm−1 was attributed to the bending stretching of NH [8,11]. Besides, the absorption peak at 1130cm−1 was asymmetric stretching vibrations of CN or CS, 1380cm−1 is assigned to the stretching of CN and 1660cm−1 was the vibration absorption peak of CO [26–27]. The FTIR spectrum of GCDs-w and GCDs-y (Fig. S2) are similar with PCDs-w and PCDs-y. Therefore, these results demonstrate that the as-prepared PCDs contain lots of CO, NH2 and COOH groups on the surfaces, as supported by the above FT-IR analysis. The functional groups endow high selectivity of PCDs for Hg2+ and Fe3+ detection.

XPS was also carried out to determine the surface contents and chemical configurations of the PCDs materials. The XPS survey spectrum in Fig. 3a reveal that the PCDs-m comprise carbon, oxygen, nitrogen and sulfur elements and it shows a predominant graphitic C 1s peak at about 284eV, a N 1s peak at about 401eV and an O 1s peak at about 532eV [7]. The high-resolution spectrum of C1s can be assigned to three surface components in the PCDs-m, corresponding to CC at binding energy of 284.8eV, CO/CS/CN at 286.3eV, as well as CO at 288.3eV (Fig. 3b). The N 1s spectrum in Fig. 3c is fitted with two peaks at 400.1eV and 401.1eV, which are ascribed to pyrrolic N and NH bonds, respectively. The S 2p spectrum in Fig. 3d shows two main bands at 168.6 and 169.8eV, which are assigned to CS and CSOx, respectively [6,26]. The XPS survey spectrum in Fig. S3a, Fig. S4a and Fig. S5a reveal that the PCDs-w, PCDs-y and PCDs-m comprise carbon, oxygen, nitrogen and sulfur elements. In addition, the sample contains of carbon, oxygen, nitrogen and other atoms at the corresponding binding energies given in Table S1. It can be found that the PCDs-m consists of many other atoms like Na, Ca, P, Cl, it may be that pigeon eats whole grains and drain a lot of K, Na, Ca and Cl elements compared with feather and egg. Therefore, all of the above results demonstrate the existence of hydroxyl and carboxylic groups on the surface of PCDs.

As shown in Fig. 4a–d, the PCDs suspensions are light yellow and transparent. And when it was excited under UV light (365nm), it exhibits strong blue emission. Fig. 4e depicts the UV–Vis and fluorescence spectra of the diluted solution of resultant PCDs in aqueous solution. In Fig. 4e, it is clearly seen that the UV–Vis spectrum shows a strong peak at around 225nm that can be ascribed to the π-π* transitions of the aromatic CC sp2 domains which can't produce observed fluorescence signal [28]. The absorption peak at 285nm of PCDs-m is from the trapping of excited state energy of the surface states, which can lead to strong fluorescence. Besides, the UV–Vis spectrum of PCDs-w shows strong peaks in the 250–300nm region and it is attributed to n-π* transition corresponding to the carbonyl/amine functional groups on the surface in PCDs-w [29]. On the other hand, fluorescence spectra depicted an excitation dependent behavior. Fig. 5a–d shows the PL spectra of PCDs with different excitation wavelengths in water. With an increase in excitation wavelength from 300 to 400nm, the maximum emission shifted from 395 to 475nm along with a concurrent decrease in emission intensity, which may be due to the optical selection of the surface functional groups in the PCDs. As shown in Fig. 5 and Fig. S6, the strongest emission peak of PCDs and GCDs is similar to located at 400nm with different excitation wavelength. The maximum excitation of PCDs-f, PCDs-w, PCDs-y, PCDs-m is 330, 340, 340 and 350nm, respectively.

As shown in Table S2 and Table 1 , with quinine sulfate as reference, the fluorescence emission quantum yields (QY) of the PCDs-f at 330nm excitation is calculated to be 24.87% much higher than goose feathers CDs previously reported [17]. The QY of the PCDs-w and PCDs-y at 340nm excitation are calculated to be 17.48% and 16.34%, respectively. The QY of the PCDs-m at 350nm excitation is calculated to be 33.5%. Among the four samples, PCDs-m possesses the highest QY, which is even higher than the other previously reported CDs derived from biomass as shown in Table 1. As illustrated in Fig. 6a, changing the pH values from 5 to 9 in solutions yields negligible effects on the fluorescence intensities of PCDs. Fig. 6b displays the effect of stabilization time on the PL intensity of PCDs, showing that the PCDs were very stable in air. All of these results confirm the efficient synthesis of water-soluble and good stable PCDs with blue photoluminescence.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence Detection for Hg2+ and Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: The sensing potential was evaluated by monitoring the initial fluorescence intensity of the PCDs for possible significant changes once added with the metal ions. Metal ions are commonly present in the environment especially in water sources and are chosen for this study, which include Ag+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Al3+, Cu2+, Hg2+, K+, Pb2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Na+, Ni2+, Fe3+, Pd2+, Ba2+, Ce3+ and Zn2+. Fig. 7a–d shows the effect upon addition of metal ions on the fluorescence intensity of the PCDs. As shown in Fig. 7a, these metal ions had different influence on the PL intensity of the PCDs-f, and the lowest PL intensity was notably achieved for the PCDs-f upon the addition of Hg2+ and Fe3+ ions. We can clearly know that the PL of PCDs-w and PCDs-y were no selective to most of the metal cations but Hg2+. However, Ag+, Cu2+ and Hg2+ ions can effectively quench the fluorescence of the PCDs-m. To evaluate the selectivity of the present sensing system, the competitive experiments were also carried out by monitoring the change in fluorescence intensity. Other cations were added to the detection experiments, and the absorbance changes with or without interfering ions were compared as shown in Fig. S7. Such results directly indicate that the PCDs sensors have high selectivity for Hg2+ and Fe3+ detection.

Besides, we compared the different kinds of eggs preparation of CDs. We found that the preparation of GCDs with goose eggs also can detect Hg2+ via pyrolysis carbonization method as shown in Fig. S8. However, Zhang et al. [7] prepared highly PL CDs deriving from fresh chicken egg white via hydrothermal strategy which can detect Fe3+, Liu et al. [17] synthesized CDs from goose feathers by a facile microwave-hydrothermal approach and the goose feather derived CDs exhibit highly sensitive and selective detection behavior of Fe3+ ions. We think that using different methods make them have different degree of carbonization and the content and composition of CDs surface functional groups may be not the same.

As described in Fig. 8 , the PL intensity of PCDs-f linearly decrease with Hg2+/Fe3+ concentrations. The fluorescence quenching data can be fitted by the Stern-Volmer equation: F0/F−1=KSVC, the detection limit of PCDs-f is about 10.3 and 60.9nM (S/N=3) for Hg2+ and Fe3+ detection, respectively. As shown in Fig. S9, the detection limit of PCDs-w and PCDs-y is 34.6 and 34.9nM for Hg2+ detection. It should be noted that the obtained limit of detection of the PCDs sensor for Hg2+ and Fe3+ detection is lower than those previously reported shown in Table 2 with other different fluorescent probes. It was found that the detection limit of PCDs-f is lower than PCDs-w and PCDs-y, because the QY of PCDs-f is higher than them (Table 1).

PCDs-f can detect Hg2+ and Fe3+ ions at the same time, in order to distinguish them, we used two kinds of buffer solution: Tris-HCl buffer and HEPES solution (pH=7.4). As shown in Fig. 9 , Hg2+ can efficiently quench the fluorescence of PCDs-f in HEPES buffer solution, while almost no quenching is detected in the case of Fe3+ under the same conditions. This is due to the fact that PCDs-f possesses a higher affinity with Tris-HCl as compared with Hg2+ during the assay process [19].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The Mechanism of the Hg2+ and Fe3+ Detection
ce:section.ce:para: Hg2+ and Fe3+ ions can specifically quench the fluorescence of the as-synthesized PCDs because of the ion selective chemical structure of PCDs and the inner filter effect. The outstanding selectivity and specificity can be probably attributed to that Hg2+ has a stronger affinity toward the carboxylic group on PCDs surface than other metal ions. The stability constants between Hg2+ and carboxylic group are higher than other metal ions [33]. By FTIR and XPS spectrum, we can find that the surface of PCDs have many carboxylic and amino groups. Thus, PCDs can detect Hg2+ and Fe3+ ions. Besides, Hg2+/Fe3+-induced fluorescence quenching may be ascribed to the electron or energy transfer process occurring between the excited PCDs and Hg2+/Fe3+. They can quench the fluorescence of PCNDs due to facilitate non-radiative electron/hole recombination annihilation through an effective electron transfer process, leading to a substantial decrease of the fluorescence of PCDs [33–35].

The PCDs were also evaluated for cytotoxic activity using an MTT assay (Fig. S10). The particles showed no cytotoxic effect on HUVEC cells. Even at the highest concentration (200μgmL−1), there was no significant variation in the cytomorphology of the treated cells when compared to the control. In view of the above-mentioned higher performance of the sensor PCDs-f, bioimaging applications of PCDs-f for monitoring of Hg2+ in living cells were carried out. After HUVEC cells were incubated with PCDs-f in culture medium for 1h and washed with fresh 1× PBS (pH=7.0) three times, the bright fluorescence was observed from within the cell (Fig. 10 A and B). PCDs-f can be observed mainly in the cell membrane and the cytoplasmic area. The cells were then supplemented with Hg2+ in the growth medium for another 30min at 37°C, and the fluorescent intensity obviously decreases (Fig. 10 C and D). These initial experiments on living cells show the great potential of CDs for fundamental biology research.

We have demonstrated a facile and green synthesis method to prepare four fluorescent nitrogen and sulfur codoped PCDs by pyrolysis reactions in pure water solution using cheap and easily obtained pigeon feather, egg white, egg yolk and manure as the reaction precursor. The as-prepared PCDs show strong and stable fluorescence, which is dependent on excitation wavelength. They have high fluorescence quantum yield about 24.87% (PCDs-f), 17.48% (PCDs-w), 16.34% (PCDs-y), 33.50% (PCDs-m), respectively, which is higher than the other CDs preparing from biomass. The prepared PCDs-f can be used for the detection of Hg2+ and Fe3+ by simply using the buffer solution of HEPES for Hg2+ with a detection limit of 10.9nM. The PCDs-w and PCDs-y can be utilized as the highly sensitive and selective detection behavior of Hg2+ ion. Thus, PCDs shows great promise for application in bioimaging and biomedicine and opens up enormous opportunities for multifunctional devices or sensors.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are grateful for the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (nos. 21174103, 21374078 and 51303132), Tianjin Research Program of Application Foundation and Advanced Technology (grant number: 15JCYBJC18100).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at doi:10.1016/j.saa.2016.10.039.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Heavy metal toxicity and the environment

Simple hydrothermal preparation of carbon nanodots and their application in colorimetric and fluorimetric detection of mercury ions

An ionic liquid promoted microwave-hydrothermal route towards highly photoluminescent carbon dots for sensitive and selective detection of iron(iii)

Fluorescent carbon nanoparticles for the fluorescent detection of metal ions
Facile synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots for Fe3+ sensing and cellular imaging
Carbon nanodots as fluorescence probes for rapid, sensitive, and label-free detection of Hg2+ and biothiols in complex matrices
